. A well-known Irish literary man, in the course of a
letter, in which he refers to the question of the attitude
of Irish Home Rulers to the Union Jack, remarks:—
“* Apart from the urgent necessity of such s oourse,
even for the humour of the thing, the Nationalists ought
_to carry Union Jacks on all excursions in Ulster.”” An-
other: gentleman, and one who enjoys & liberal income
‘“under the Government,”” and who no doubé looks
forward to rapid and well-earned promotion, says in
connection with this matter: ““ I prefer my prejudices
to my principles. Reason be damned.”” Why not
then give up his Government ‘‘ sit.”” and go ‘‘ on his
keeping ”’ in the Dublin mountains!

The question of the Union Jack raised by us last week
has excited a great deal of interest: no doubt ik came
as a shock to many, particularly some of our desperate
“‘rebels *’ in his Majesty’s Civil Service. Our artist
with a humorous pencil hits off one side of the matter.
As will be seen, he pictures a procession of A.0.H.
men, or ‘‘Molly Maguires,”” as Conciliating William
loves to call them, marching with a Union Jaek and
enjoying the humour of the situation immensely, whilst
the flag that the Orangemen very profitably use as a
pardy badge actually falls from the unnerved hand of
the Orange ringleader when he beholds wltat the ‘‘Molly
Maguires *’ are at; and the expression on the faces of
the other Orange lambs express very well the feelings
with which these glim treason-mongers who masque-
wads fairly successfully before the Big Brother as the
only ‘‘loyalists,”” would experience in such circum-
stances.

. We think we were the first to point out some years
ago the-fallacy in one sense of regarding ourselves as
the majority in this country. Being struck with our
tameness and apparent want of spirit in many direc-
tions, and speculating on our apparent lack of back-
bone in contrast with the historic fact that after all the
centuries we were not conquered yet, we found the
explanation .in the fact—a fact inclined to be ignored
even by reflecting men by reason of our being s nume-
zical majority in Ireland—that we are a very imsigni-
ficant numerical minority in the entity that in practice
counts—the entity of Great Britain and Ireland. In
1911 there were nearly 41,000,000 people in England,
Wales and Scotland, and in Ireland there were only
nearly 4,400,000. Of the Irish population at least one
million may be classed as anti-National, and so but
with a little over 8,000,000 people we are a compara-
tively very small numerieal fact in a total population
of ‘about-45,000,000. The million Unionists have the
of the Big Brother and they are fairly.vigorous in
eir campaign of tattling to him. If a row occurred

in which a Unionisb tooth is knocked out, the Big Bro-
ther gets cartloads of telegrams about it, even though
the Unionist may have deserved his punishment, and
have given great provocation. The Big Brother only
hears of the "¢ outrage’’ on the tooth of the son of the
Union Jack. But if a Nationalist 1s'assuul(;ed the news
of that assault finds itself up against a s_orb of stop
trap. We had concrete instances of this in the first
Castledawson affair, when the Big Brother was over-
loaded with lies against Nationalists in connection with
that scufile, and in the case where the Dungannon
Nationalist journalist was foully assaulted and almost
done to death by Orange blackguards, and the truth of
which episode found great difficulty even in leaking out
in driblets to the Big Brother. Similarly no matter
what amount of anti-Christian bigotry 1s practised
against Catholics in this country, there is no Amarke’?
for the news even amongst the so-called ‘‘ Liberal

organs of Great Britain, but if an urchin said boo to
a non-Catholic street preacher, or if some man, carried
away by natural indignation, kicked a Souper or struck
a Jumper in the face, all the newsagencies in Great
Britain, *“ Liberal ’ and Conservative, are anxious for
the information, and the Big Brother gets a great feed
of *“ Roman Catholic intolerance in Ireland.”” We axe
a very small minority in the entity that under present
circumstances count. We have to get Home Rule
under these circumstances. For decades we have been
*‘ proving our tolerance,”” and our readers know our
views abbut the efficacy of that policy. Three big
things that operate against Ireland in the world of Greab
Britain opinion is that- mere Ireland is Catholic, that
Ireland is rebel amd anti-English, and that there is a
loyal minority, Iingland’'s Faithful Garrison in Ireland,
holding the fort in this country as the only true and

real loyal subjects of the King. - The mere Irish will- -

ingly talke all the material disadvantages consequent on
their being Catholics and there is no room for an iota of
change or compromise there. But why play into the
hands of the Orangemen by keeping up the falsehood—
for it has become that though only few recognise it—of
posing as rebels, however qualified, in contradistinction
to the loyal Orangemen, when, as we pointed out years
ago, we are not only loyal but tame at that. We know
a type of Civil Servant who is so red hot ‘‘ National ”’
that he could call us names for reasoning in this cold
fashion, but we have discarded that most ridiculous of
all our ridiculous types of bogus “‘ rebels’ from our
calculations long ago: he and his Itkes have retired to
that comparative oblivion where all that can be said
of them is that their peculiarities call for the investiga-
tion of -psychalogists |

tion for Home Rule, are asked to believe that the
Orange rabble are the real loyalists in Ireland,

Servant and director of the ‘‘ Freeman,”’ know about
Arg or Art Galleries? On this Art Gallery nuisance he
is becoming a very Dr. McWalter at newspaper letter-

The Orange procession went trooping along,
With truculent music and Protestant song;
With Union Jack flaunting and drum in full play
The sons of King Billy went forth on that day.

 They blustered, and threatened, and cursed as they
went ° /
Home Rule and the Pope to their bosom’s content.
They vowed to defend Constitution and State
" From Papists who loyalty’s banner do hate.

“* Ye rebelly Papists who dare to draw near, = . .
Come gaze ab our flag and then tremble with fear.
Hope: not for Home Rule while the Orangemen brave
Their Union Jack banner. of loyalty wave.

“ Oh mon, it is fine to be loyal and true

- And have England’s flag on the side of our crew;
’Pis grand that we roughs have excuses to brag
Of loyal allegiance to empire and flag.

8o }.lbisﬁing our loyt;.l advertisement gay
We bang the big'drum, and we sing Dolly’s Brae,

" And never was scen such a spiritless pack

And Romanist rebels who green banners bear
Of our loyal feelings had better beware."

“But soon, as it happened, did this Orange flock

Encounter some Papists who gave them a shock, 2
A smiling procession of Papists who waved

The flag of the Empire the same as the  Saved.”

In wild consternation the Orangemen stopped
And down was their banner of loyalty dropped.:
With rage and dismay, with disgust and despair,

THE Irish public are heartily sick of strikes and

rumours of strikes, and the sympathetic strike
is an unworkable policy. Labour of all classes and
degrees bound together in sympathy and ready to put
its full force on the labour side in any dispute is merely
the class war; it is bad economics, bad sense and un-
workable. We have no liking for the organised sym-
pathetic strike. The svmp'\thom, stul\o, one would
have thought, received its death-blow in Ireland when
a dispute “in thc Dublin timber trade precipitated a
disastrous railway strike in Ireland, as a result of which
many of the non-combatants suffered severely and
many of the men were badly hit.

dent "’ we need not go, but we don’t agree with
the agitation that would attempt to forbid the sale of
the ““ Independent '’ merely because there is a labour
dispute of one kind or other affecting a section of the
staff. By all means let labour men advise their fol-
lowers not to buy Murphy’s paper if they feel inclined
to give that advice, but that is a different matter from
attempting to limit the liberty of traders from trading
in it. Labour disputes in firms or industries when
unhappily they occur should be isolated as much as pos-
We could understand, and for our
part we would sympathise with a movement that
attempts to bring as.much legitimate pressure as pos-
sible against the sale of printed stuff-on the ground of
decency and morality. That is a clear and clean issue;
but our judgment is against an effort to forcibly stop

strotches of bogland, the stone-walled fields, the clusters
of white cottages, the sweeps of pasture or harvest
land. They have their own charm and character, and:
their fault is that they are, porhaps, too simple, and allow
no scope for architectural genius, and have their beauty
rather in the absence of ugliness than in the presence oft
any. peculiar artistic merit. Hence such churches may
be too poor and thin w presentiment of the Irish religious
sentiment, and others in harmony with the old spirit but of
better design, would be more worthy of the race and its
history.

To build the ideal church is not an impossibility—
indeed an ideal church alrcady exists, and can be seen
to-day in the Island of Inishbofin, off the Galway Coast.

was but laid on Scptember Sth, 1910, and in a few cCays

St. Colman. Every thinghas been thought out in its
building—the climate, the landscape, the stormy weather,
the traditions of the fisher-folk—and the result is & most
perfect liftle church that will remain a lasting memorial

centre ol the straggling village of Middle Quarter,
